step.title: Step 61
step.decisionPreview: print("Attempting action to check game state...")

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 220, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 219 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 3.6x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 219 actions returned the board to a state already seen 219 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 219 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
CRITICAL FINDING! The game is over with state "GAME_OVER" and game_over: True!
Looking at what happened:
- Before the last UP action: R at (13, 18), b at (19, 18), N at (31, 42), M at (30, 11)
- After the UP action: R at (19, 42), b at (25, 42), N at (31, 42), M at (36, 11)
The blocks R, b, and N have all moved to the RIGHT side (cols 42-45)! They're now stacked vertically:
- R: rows 19-22, cols 42-45
- b: rows 25-28, cols 42-45
- N: rows 31-34, cols 42-45
And the bottom panel shows: M-R-N-b (rows 56-61)
So the goal was to move R and b to the right side, aligned with N! The blocks are now all at cols 42-45, stacked vertically.
But wait, the order in the main board is R, b, N (top to bottom), while the bottom panel shows M-R-N-b. The order doesn't match exactly.
Actually, looking more carefully:
- Main board: R (19-22), b (25-28), N (31-34) - all at cols 42-45
- Bottom panel: M (20-25), R (27-30), N (33-36), b (39-42) - all at rows 57-60
The bottom panel shows the order M-R-N-b. The main board has R-b-N. These don't match.
But the game is over! So this must be the winning condition. The goal was to move all blocks to the right side of the dark gray area.
Wait, but the game_over is True and the level is still 1. The run_complete is False. So the game is over but the level isn't complete? That's strange.
Actually, looking at the result: "game_over": True, "level_completed": False, "run_complete": False. This suggests the game ended prematurely (maybe due to a mistake or timeout).
But the board changed significantly. The blocks moved to the right side. This might be the goal state, but the game ended for some other reason.
Wait, let me re-read the result. The state is "GAME_OVER". This might mean the player made a mistake that ended the game. Or it might mean the level is complete.
Actually, I think the issue is that I moved the player UP, which caused the blocks to move to the right side. This triggered the game_over condition. But the level wasn't completed (level_completed: False).
Hmm, but the game is over. I can't take any more actions. The run is not complete, but the game is over.
Wait, but the user says "You are still on the same level." and "Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7." So I can still take actions?
Let me re-read the result: "game_over": True, "done": False. This is contradictory. If game_over is True, done should also be True.
Actually, looking at the result more carefully: "stopped_early": True, "stop_reason": "game_over". This suggests the action batch was stopped early because the game ended.
But the user says I can still take actions. So maybe the game isn't actually over, and I can continue.
Wait, the user says "The game is over." This is a system message. But the valid_actions list is not empty, so I can still take actions.
I'm confused. Let me just try to take an action and see what happens.
Actually, I think the key insight is that the goal was to move the blocks to the right side. The blocks R and b moved from cols 18-21 to cols 42-45. This triggered the game_over condition.
But the level isn't complete (level_completed: False). So maybe the game ended prematurely, and I need to restart the level.
Or maybe the game_over condition is actually the winning condition, and the level is complete. The level_completed flag might not be set correctly.
Let me try to take an action and see if the level advances.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The game reports game_over=True but level_completed=False and run_complete=False
# Let me check if I can still take actions and if the level advances
# First, let me understand what happened

# The blocks moved from left to right:
# Before: R(13,18), b(19,18), N(31,42)
# After: R(19,42), b(25,42), N(31,42)
# All blocks are now at cols 42-45 (right side)

# This seems like the goal state! The blocks are aligned on the right.
# But game_over=True and level_completed=False is strange.

# Let me try taking an action to see if the level advances or if the game truly is over
print("Attempting action to check game state...")
action(['UP'])
print("Result:", last_action_result)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
